results.possibleIdentity: Per Biorn (1937–2015) was a Danish-born electrical and mechanical engineer who was a central figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and a long-time employee of Bell Telephone Laboratories. Born in Denmark, Biorn studied radar in the military before emigrating to the United States in 1962. In 1964, he was hired by Ken D. Smith to work at Bell Labs in Murray Hill, New Jersey, focusing on semiconductor research. Initially a Technical Aide working on test gear for tunnel diodes, he was promoted to Senior Technical Aide and worked alongside luminaries like William Boyle and George Smith during the development of the charge-coupled device (CCD). Biorn became one of the most prolific engineers involved with E.A.T., serving as a bridge between high technology and the arts. His notable collaborations include the 1966 '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' with artists Yvonne Rainer and Lucinda Childs, the construction of Marta Minujín's interactive 'Minuphone' (1967), and the technical realization of Lillian Schwartz's kinetic sculpture 'Proxima Centauri' (1968). He also worked with Robert Rauschenberg on projects like the 'Astral Convertible' and helped develop the Theatre Electronic Environmental Modulator (TEEM) control system.

Born in Denmark, Biorn studied radar in the military before emigrating to the United States in 1962.
In 1964, he was hired by Ken D. Smith to work at Bell Labs in Murray Hill, New Jersey, focusing on semiconductor research.

Initially a Technical Aide working on test gear for tunnel diodes, he was promoted to Senior Technical Aide and worked alongside luminaries like William Boyle and George Smith during the development of the charge-coupled device (CCD).
Biorn became one of the most prolific engineers involved with E.A.T., serving as a bridge between high technology and the arts.
